questions.text: I believe that objectivity in the face of suffering is the quality that enables me to judge when a proposed action will genuinely help and when it will not.
I feel that spending on myself is only justified after everyone around me is taken care of.
I test new approaches on a small scale before rolling them out widely.
I enjoy the weight of responsibility that comes with leading.
I rarely cry, not because I suppress emotions but because they simply don't reach that intensity.
I can still feel the emotional residue of an argument hours or even days later.
I hold the view that suffering that goes unaddressed when I had the capacity to respond is a form of moral failure that I take seriously.
I believe that how you do anything is how you do everything, so I attend to details.
I feel most effective when responsibilities are negotiated openly among team members.
I schedule thinking time into my calendar rather than leaving reflection to chance.
I find that the moral demand of suffering is the experience that makes my sense of ethical purpose feel alive and evolving rather than fixed and settled.
I find social situations most comfortable when I know exactly what is expected of me.
I find that fixing something in someone else's home or workspace is deeply gratifying.
I notice when two people in a group have unspoken friction between them.
I believe that my personal energy is a finite resource that I should allocate with deliberate intention toward my own goals.
I find that waiting for important news doesn't produce the anxiety I see in others.
I sense when someone is listening with the intent to find a weakness in what is being said.
I feel that the moral pull I experience in the presence of suffering is a signal I should trust, not manage.
I believe it is better to overcommit to helping others than to undercommit.
My digital files end up wherever is convenient; I search when I need something.
I find that others naturally look to me for direction.
I hold the view that composure during painful circumstances is a quality whose true worth only becomes apparent when the crisis is over and the consequences of decisions become clear.
I think that the professional skill of remaining emotionally neutral during difficult interactions is one that can be learned and strengthened with practice.
I tend to choose the apartment, the neighborhood, and the city that best serve my own goals.
I enjoy the challenge of getting skeptics to follow my lead.
I find that I can switch between emotional contexts without carrying residue from one to the next.
I tend to miss the moment when a conversation shifts from casual to serious.
I want to support whoever launches new ventures within my team.
I respond to messages when the timing feels right, not according to a self-imposed schedule.
I focus on ideas and content rather than on reading people.
I am patient in meetings that take longer than expected, trusting the process.
A messy desk doesn't bother me; I know where everything is in my own way.
I pick up on the subtle cues that indicate someone feels out of place in a group.
I feel that there is nothing wrong with making myself the center of my own life.
I experience the surge of emotion after a close call — almost missing a flight, nearly dropping something precious — as lasting and intense.
I notice that my face and body betray my emotional intensity before I have a chance to modulate it.
